@@ -184,7 +184,7 @@ {"id":"remote_compilation_helper-rj4.2","title":"Docs: Consolidate Configuration Precedence Documentation","description":"## Problem Configuration precedence is documented in pieces across README, config files, and help text. Users can't find a single source explaining how all configuration layers interact. ## Solution Create docs/guides/configuration.md with complete config guide: ## Contents 1. **Configuration Overview** - What can be configured - Where config files live - Precedence order diagram 2. **Precedence Layers (lowest to highest)** - Built-in defaults - User config: ~/.config/rch/config.toml - Project config: .rch/config.toml - Environment variables: RCH_* - Command-line flags 3. **Config File Reference** - daemon.toml: all fields with descriptions - workers.toml: all fields with descriptions - config.toml: all sections and fields 4. **Environment Variables** - Complete list of RCH_* variables - What each variable controls - Examples 5. **Per-Project Configuration** - When to use .rch/config.toml - What can be overridden - What cannot be overridden (security) 6. **Debugging Configuration** - rch config show - rch config show --sources - Common configuration issues ## Files to Create - docs/guides/configuration.md ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] All config options documented - [ ] Precedence clearly explained with examples - [ ] Environment variables listed - [ ] Debugging section included - [ ] Cross-referenced from README ","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-17T10:16:43.881804097-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-17T16:20:22.999566973-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-17T16:20:22.999566973-05:00","close_reason":"Added configuration guide covering precedence, config files, env vars, and debugging; linked from README.","labels":["config","docs"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"remote_compilation_helper-rj4.2","depends_on_id":"remote_compilation_helper-rj4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-17T10:16:43.928381205-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"},{"issue_id":"remote_compilation_helper-rj4.2","depends_on_id":"remote_compilation_helper-f0t.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-17T10:19:33.917440406-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]} {"id":"remote_compilation_helper-rj4.3","title":"Docs: Add Circuit Breaker Behavior Documentation","description":"## Problem Users see "circuit: open" in status output and don't understand: - What is a circuit breaker? - What causes it to open? - How long until recovery? - What actions can they take? ## Solution Add circuit breaker documentation to TROUBLESHOOTING.md and create reference guide: ## Contents 1. **What is a Circuit Breaker?** - Pattern explanation (prevent cascading failures) - How RCH uses it (per-worker protection) - Analogy: electrical circuit breaker 2. **Circuit States** - CLOSED: Normal operation, accepting jobs - OPEN: Worker failing, rejecting jobs - HALF_OPEN: Testing if worker recovered 3. **State Transitions** - Closed → Open: X consecutive failures - Open → Half-Open: Timeout elapsed - Half-Open → Closed: Probe succeeded - Half-Open → Open: Probe failed 4. **Configuration** - failure_threshold (default: 3) - success_threshold (default: 2) - timeout_secs (default: 60) 5. **What To Do** - Wait: Auto-recovery in 60s - Manual reset: rch workers reset - Investigate: Check worker SSH, logs ## Add to TROUBLESHOOTING.md Section: "Worker shows 'circuit: open'" - What it means - How to check why - How to recover ## Files to Modify - docs/TROUBLESHOOTING.md - add circuit breaker section - docs/guides/circuit-breaker.md - detailed reference ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Circuit breaker concept explained - [ ] All states documented - [ ] Transitions clearly shown - [ ] Recovery actions documented - [ ] Configuration options listed ","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-17T10:16:59.081549371-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-17T16:12:57.573915793-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-17T16:12:57.573915793-05:00","close_reason":"Expanded troubleshooting section and added circuit breaker guide with states, transitions, defaults, and recovery steps.","labels":["docs","workers"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"remote_compilation_helper-rj4.3","depends_on_id":"remote_compilation_helper-rj4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-17T10:16:59.116941108-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"},{"issue_id":"remote_compilation_helper-rj4.3","depends_on_id":"remote_compilation_helper-8qc.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-17T10:19:48.586844142-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]} {"id":"remote_compilation_helper-rj4.4","title":"Docs: Add 'How to Verify RCH is Working' Guide","description":"## Problem RCH's hook is intentionally silent for transparency. But new users can't tell if it's working. "My build took 5 seconds - was that local or remote?" ## Solution Add "Verifying RCH is Working" section to QUICKSTART and create reference: ## Contents 1. **Quick Checks** - `rch status` - shows daemon and workers - `rch status --jobs` - shows active compilations - `rch hook status` - shows if hook installed 2. **Watching Builds in Real-Time** - `rch status --watch` - live monitoring - Web dashboard at http://localhost:3000 - TUI dashboard: `rch dashboard` 3. **Build History** - `rch history` - shows recent builds - `rch history --filter remote` - only remote builds - `rch history --stats` - success rate, timing 4. **Verbose Mode** - `RCH_VISIBILITY=summary cargo build` - Shows "[RCH] Compiled on worker-1 (2.3s)" - Configurable in config.toml 5. **Testing the Hook** - `rch hook test` - simulate classification - `rch diagnose "cargo build"` - full diagnosis 6. **Signs It's Working** - Builds faster than local - `rch status` shows active builds - Worker slots decrease during builds 7. **Signs It's Not Working** - All builds take same time as before - `rch status` shows no daemon - Workers unreachable ## Files to Modify - docs/QUICKSTART.md - add verification section - docs/guides/verification.md - detailed reference ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Quick verification commands documented - [ ] Verbose mode explained - [ ] Signs of working/not working listed - [ ] Monitoring options shown - [ ] Added to QUICKSTART ","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-17T10:17:14.541828188-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-17T14:51:28.895926896-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-17T14:51:28.895926896-05:00","close_reason":"Added verification guide + Quickstart section for confirming RCH operation","labels":["docs","onboarding"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"remote_compilation_helper-rj4.4","depends_on_id":"remote_compilation_helper-rj4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-17T10:17:14.573138444-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"},{"issue_id":"remote_compilation_helper-rj4.4","depends_on_id":"remote_compilation_helper-pm5.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-17T10:19:47.910777529-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"},{"issue_id":"remote_compilation_helper-rj4.4","depends_on_id":"remote_compilation_helper-8qc.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-17T10:31:23.62563158-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]} -{"id":"remote_compilation_helper-rj4.5","title":"Docs: Add 'What is a Worker?' Infrastructure Guide","description":"## Problem Documentation assumes users know what a "build server" or "worker" is. New users ask: - What kind of machine do I need? - Can I use a cloud VM? - What should I install on it? - How many workers do I need? ## Solution Create docs/guides/worker-infrastructure.md: ## Contents 1. **What is a Worker?** - Any machine accessible via SSH - Runs compilation commands on your behalf - Returns compiled artifacts 2. **Worker Requirements** - SSH access (key-based auth) - Same architecture as local machine (x86_64 to x86_64) - Rust toolchain (matching nightly version) - Sufficient CPU cores (4+ recommended) - Sufficient RAM (8GB+ recommended) - Fast network connection 3. **Worker Options** - Cloud VMs: AWS EC2, GCP Compute, Azure VM - On-premise servers - Another workstation on LAN - Dedicated build server 4. **Cloud VM Recommendations** - AWS: c6i.2xlarge (8 vCPU, $0.34/hr) - GCP: c2-standard-8 (8 vCPU) - Spot/preemptible for cost savings 5. **Setting Up a Worker** - Install Rust nightly - Install rch-wkr binary - Configure SSH access - Test with rch workers probe 6. **Multiple Workers** - When to add more workers - Geographic distribution - Priority and load balancing ## Files to Create - docs/guides/worker-infrastructure.md ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Worker concept explained simply - [ ] Requirements clearly listed - [ ] Cloud and on-prem options covered - [ ] Cost estimates included - all with zstd compression support. 4 tests pass."} {"id":"remote_compilation_helper-sce","title":"Task: Benchmark History Time-Series Chart","description":"## Overview Implement an interactive time-series chart showing SpeedScore history over time, allowing users to visualize performance trends and identify changes. ## Background and Justification Performance can change due to: - Hardware degradation - System updates - Network changes - Shared resource contention Historical visualization helps identify when changes occurred and correlate with events. ## Implementation Details ### Chart Component ```tsx interface BenchmarkHistoryChartProps { workerId: string; history: SpeedScoreHistoryEntry[]; dateRange: [Date, Date]; onDateRangeChange: (range: [Date, Date]) => void; showComponents?: boolean; } interface SpeedScoreHistoryEntry { measured_at: Date; total: number; cpu_score: number; memory_score: number; disk_score: number; network_score: number; compilation_score: number; } ``` ### Chart Features 1. **Main Line**: Total SpeedScore over time 2. **Component Lines**: Toggle individual components (CPU, Memory, etc.) 3. **Date Range Selector**: Zoom to specific time periods 4. **Hover Tooltip**: Show exact values at any point 5. **Annotations**: Mark significant events (system updates, config changes) ### Using Recharts (React-native) ```tsx import { LineChart, Line, XAxis, YAxis, Tooltip, Legend, ResponsiveContainer, Brush } from 'recharts'; const BenchmarkHistoryChart: React.FC = ({ history, showComponents = false, }) => { return ( format(date, 'MMM d')} /> } /> {showComponents && ( <> )} ); }; ``` ### Date Range Presets ```tsx const DATE_RANGE_PRESETS = [ { label: '24h', days: 1 }, { label: '7d', days: 7 }, { label: '30d', days: 30 }, { label: '90d', days: 90 }, { label: 'All', days: null }, ]; ``` ### Custom Tooltip ```tsx const CustomTooltip = ({ active, payload, label }) => { if (!active || !payload) return null; return (

{format(label, 'PPpp')}

{payload.map((entry) => (

{entry.name}: {entry.value.toFixed(1)}

))}
); }; ``` ### Performance Considerations - Limit data points (downsample if >1000 points) - Use WebWorker for data processing - Virtualize tooltip for large datasets - Lazy load chart library ## Comparison Mode Add ability to overlay multiple workers: ```tsx interface ComparisonChartProps { workers: Array<{ workerId: string; history: SpeedScoreHistoryEntry[]; color: string; }>; } ``` ## Dependencies - Requires SpeedScore history API endpoint - Part of Web Dashboard SpeedScore Integration epic - Recharts library (add to package.json) ## Testing Requirements - Unit tests for data transformation - Snapshot tests for chart rendering - Interaction tests for tooltips, zoom - Performance tests with large datasets ## Files to Create/Modify - `web/components/BenchmarkHistoryChart.tsx` - `web/components/ChartTooltip.tsx` - `web/hooks/useSpeedScoreHistory.ts` - `web/utils/chartDataTransform.ts` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Displays SpeedScore over time - [ ] Toggle individual components - [ ] Date range selection with presets - [ ] Interactive tooltips with exact values - [ ] Smooth rendering with 1000+ data points - [ ] Responsive design - [ ] Export chart as image (optional)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-17T10:51:00.816707657-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-17T10:51:00.816707657-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"remote_compilation_helper-sce","depends_on_id":"remote_compilation_helper-y8n","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-17T10:56:24.632896008-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]} {"id":"remote_compilation_helper-scs","title":"Build Cancellation Support","description":"## Problem Once a build starts on a remote worker, there is no way to cancel it: - User realizes they started the wrong build - Build is taking too long and user wants to try locally - Worker appears stuck and user wants to abort - User needs the worker for a more urgent build Currently, users must wait for the build to complete or kill processes manually. ## Solution Add build cancellation to CLI and web dashboard. ### CLI: rch cancel ``` $ rch queue ACTIVE BUILDS ID PROJECT WORKER ELAPSED b-a1b2 myproject gpu-server-1 5m 15s $ rch cancel b-a1b2 Cancelling build b-a1b2 on gpu-server-1... ✓ Sent SIGTERM to remote cargo process ✓ Cleaned up partial artifacts ✓ Released worker slot Build cancelled. $ rch cancel --all Cancel all 3 active builds? [y/N] y Cancelled 3 builds. ``` ### Web Dashboard - Cancel button on each active build in queue panel - Confirmation dialog for cancel - Visual feedback during cancellation ## Implementation Details ### Cancellation Flow ``` 1. User requests cancel (CLI or web) 2. Daemon sends cancel signal to worker 3. Worker: a. Sends SIGTERM to cargo process b. Waits 5 seconds for graceful shutdown c. Sends SIGKILL if still running d. Cleans up target/ artifacts (optional) e. Reports cancellation complete 4. Daemon: a. Marks build as cancelled b. Releases worker slot c. Notifies queue subscribers ``` ### API Endpoints - `POST /api/builds/{id}/cancel` - Cancel specific build - `POST /api/builds/cancel-all` - Cancel all active builds ### Graceful vs Force Cancel ```rust pub enum CancelMode { /// Send SIGTERM, wait for graceful shutdown Graceful { timeout: Duration }, /// Send SIGKILL immediately Force, } ``` ### Partial Artifact Cleanup On cancel, optionally clean up: - Partial compilation artifacts in target/ - Incomplete rsync transfers - Temporary files This prevents corrupted incremental compilation state. ## Testing Requirements ### Unit Tests ```rust #[test] fn test_cancel_sends_sigterm() { info!("TEST START: test_cancel_sends_sigterm"); let build = start_test_build(); info!("INPUT: Active build {}", build.id); let result = cancel_build(&build.id, CancelMode::Graceful { timeout: Duration::from_secs(5) }); info!("RESULT: Cancel result: {:?}", result); assert!(result.is_ok()); assert!(result.unwrap().process_terminated); info!("TEST PASS: test_cancel_sends_sigterm"); } ``` ### E2E Tests - Cancel graceful completes within timeout - Cancel force terminates immediately - Cancelled builds dont appear in history as success - Worker becomes available after cancel ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] CLI can cancel individual or all builds - [ ] Web dashboard has cancel buttons - [ ] Graceful cancel waits for cargo to finish current unit - [ ] Force cancel terminates immediately - [ ] Partial artifacts cleaned up by default - [ ] Worker slot released after cancel","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-17T11:22:53.07224587-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-17T11:22:53.07224587-05:00"}

@@ -0,0 +1,79 @@
+# Worker Infrastructure Guide
+
+This guide explains what a "worker" is, what kind of machine you need, and how to choose or provision workers for RCH.
+
+## What is a Worker?
+
+A worker is any machine that:
+- Is reachable over SSH from your workstation.
+- Runs compilation commands on your behalf.
+- Returns build artifacts back to your local project.
+
+In practice, a worker is a build server. It can be a cloud VM, an on-prem server, or another workstation on your network.
+
+## Worker Requirements
+
+Minimum requirements:
+- Linux machine with SSH access (key-based auth).
+- Same CPU architecture as your workstation (e.g., x86_64 -> x86_64, aarch64 -> aarch64).
+- Rust toolchain that matches your local nightly (see `rust-toolchain.toml`).
+- At least 4 CPU cores.
+- At least 8 GB RAM.
+- Reliable network connection to your workstation.
+
+Recommended for best performance:
+- 8+ CPU cores.
+- 16+ GB RAM.
+- NVMe SSD.
+- Low-latency, high-bandwidth network (LAN or good cloud region proximity).
+
+## Worker Options
+
+Common choices:
+- **Cloud VMs**: AWS EC2, GCP Compute, Azure VM.
+- **On-prem servers**: spare rack servers or lab machines.
+- **Another workstation**: a second dev box on the same LAN.
+- **Dedicated build server**: a small cluster or a single powerful machine.
+
+If you already have SSH access to a Linux machine, it can be a worker.
+
+## Cloud VM Recommendations (Ballpark)
+
+These examples are typical in the 8 vCPU range. Pricing varies by region and time, so treat these as rough estimates:
+- **AWS**: c6i.2xlarge (8 vCPU) ~ $0.30-$0.40/hr on-demand.
+- **GCP**: c2-standard-8 (8 vCPU) ~ $0.30-$0.45/hr on-demand.
+- **Azure**: F8s v2 (8 vCPU) ~ $0.30-$0.50/hr on-demand.
+
+Tips:
+- Use spot/preemptible instances for cost savings if occasional interruptions are OK.
+- Prefer regions close to your workstation to reduce latency.
+
+## Setting Up a Worker (High-Level)
+
+1. **Install Rust nightly** on the worker.
+2. **Install RCH worker binary (`rch-wkr`)** on the worker.
+3. **Configure SSH access** from your workstation.
+4. **Add the worker to RCH** and probe it.
+
+Detailed setup instructions:
+- Worker setup and config: `docs/guides/workers.md`
+- SSH setup: `docs/guides/ssh-setup.md`
+
+## Multiple Workers
+
+Add more workers when:
+- You run multiple agents in parallel.
+- Your builds regularly saturate CPU.
+- You want redundancy if a worker goes down.
+
+Guidance:
+- Start with 1 worker and scale up as needed.
+- Prefer a few strong workers over many weak ones.
+- Use priorities to steer builds to faster machines.
+- Spread workers geographically only if network latency is acceptable.
+
+## Next Steps
+
+- Set up your first worker: `docs/guides/workers.md`
+- Validate connectivity: `rch workers probe --all`
+- Monitor worker health: `rch status --workers`
